summary: The manuscript's primary redundancy pattern is systematic re-introduction of characters' physical descriptors across chapters, especially in adjacent or near-adjacent chapters. Lola's silver-hair-plus-gold-crucifix pairing (25+ instances), Earl's large hands, Burt's wispy-white-hair-plus-musician's-fingers, Agnes's wire-rimmed-glasses-plus-gray-hair, and Harry's faded blue cardigan (11 instances) are all re-established multiple times beyond their first introduction. The TV room location is described near-verbatim in Ch3 and Ch5. Proof-of-baseline accumulation is heavy for Earl's cough (32+ instances across 14 chapters), Burt's 'dementia was real' (4 near-verbatim restatements), and Dr. Prasad's 'forty patients a day across three facilities' (near-verbatim repeat). Em-dash density is 18.6% manuscript-wide (422/2269 prose lines; threshold 10%), with Ch15 at 47.6% and Ch11 at 37.4%. 'She' sentence openers reach 12.8% of all sentences (778/6069; threshold 7%) and 'something' appears 155 times (1 per 529 prose words; threshold 1 per 800) — noted but not emitted as per-chapter findings to preserve the findings budget for structural redundancy and consistency defects. Consistency audit found three critical naming contradictions: Room 110's patient changes from Delvecchio (Ch1) to Davila (Ch2) to Delacroix (Ch4+); Agnes's roommate changes from Nowak (Ch1) to Dorothy (Ch2) to Doris Okonkwo (Ch8+) to Mrs. Delacroix (Ch17, colliding with the Room 110 patient name); and Burt's instrument is described as piano (Ch3, Ch9) but trumpet (Ch11, continuity sheet). Timeline, geography, financial figures, room numbers, and key object tracking are otherwise consistent.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1 L17: 'Lola's silver hair was loose on her shoulders...The gold crucifix at her throat caught the light from the television'; Ch2 L244: 'her silver hair freshly brushed — Tammy would've done it on the evening round — and her gold crucifix catching the last daylight through the blinds'
redundancy_findings.problem: Lola's two-trait descriptor pairing (silver hair + gold crucifix) is re-established in Ch2 one chapter after Ch1's full introduction at L17. The reader already has both details from the previous chapter.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Remove or replace the silver-hair-plus-gold-crucifix pairing in Ch2 L244. Enter the scene through Lola's behavior, posture, or the specific emotional register of her greeting — not through traits the reader learned last chapter. A single identifying detail (e.g., the tremor, the recliner, the game-show glow) is sufficient.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch2's first Lola passage does not mention both silver hair and gold crucifix. At most one physical trait from Ch1's introduction may appear, and only in passing.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1 L51: 'Earl was tall even folded into the chair, with hands large enough to make the magazine look like a pamphlet'; Ch3 L431: 'Earl Washington was eighty-one, six-two when he stood straight...He had large hands, a steelworker's hands'; Ch3 L654: 'Earl's stooped back, his large hands steady on the chair'
redundancy_findings.problem: Earl's height and large hands are established in Ch1 L51. Ch3 re-introduces him with full name, age, exact height (six-two), and 'large hands, a steelworker's hands' — a fuller version of the same traits. The 'large hands' descriptor appears again at L654 in the same chapter.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch3 L431, cut the height restatement and the 'large hands, a steelworker's hands' phrase. Enter Earl through his action (pushing Burt's wheelchair) and his operational silence, which are the new information this chapter provides. At L654, 'his large hands steady on the chair' can stay as a single callback but does not need the second 'large hands' mention at L431.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch3's first description of Earl does not re-state his height or hand size. His entry is defined by action and demeanor, not physical re-inventory.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1 L57: 'wispy white hair pressed flat on one side...His hands lay on top of the blanket, long and thin, with the fine bones of someone who'd done precise, delicate work'; Ch3 L433: 'his wispy white hair was going in three directions and his long fingers — musician's fingers, Agnes called them'
redundancy_findings.problem: Burt's wispy white hair and long/fine-boned fingers are established in Ch1 L57. Ch3 L433 re-describes both traits two chapters later with synonymous phrasing ('wispy white hair,' 'long fingers'). The musician's-fingers gloss is new but the underlying physical observation is redundant.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch3 L433, cut 'wispy white hair was going in three directions' — the reader already knows Burt's hair. Keep 'musician's fingers' as a new characterization by Agnes but drop the prior physical description. Enter Burt through his state (asleep, chin on chest, fingers twitching) rather than re-cataloguing his appearance.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch3's first Burt passage does not repeat 'wispy white hair.' The musician's-fingers label may remain as new information from Agnes, but the hair and hand-shape re-inventory is gone.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1 L41: 'She was small and thin, gray hair cut short, wire-rimmed glasses reflecting the slant of hallway light'; Ch3 L403: 'her wire-rimmed glasses sat low on her nose because the left arm was bent...seventy-six and weighed maybe a hundred and ten pounds, and she moved through the room with the authority of forty-two years at the Walgreens in Edison'
redundancy_findings.problem: Agnes's wire-rimmed glasses and small stature are established in Ch1 L41. Ch3 L403 re-introduces the glasses and adds weight and age, but the glasses + small-frame pairing is redundant for the reader who met Agnes two chapters ago.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch3 L403, cut or trim the wire-rimmed glasses reference — one mention in Ch1 is sufficient until much later. The pharmacist-authority characterization and the bent glasses arm are new and can stay. Lead with Agnes's operational behavior (arriving first, carrying notebooks, two-pen system) rather than physical re-inventory.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch3's Agnes entry does not re-describe wire-rimmed glasses if that detail appeared in Ch1's introduction. The pharmacist authority framing may remain.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1 L41: 'small and thin, gray hair cut short, wire-rimmed glasses'; Ch8 L1583: 'She kept her gray hair short and cut it herself with nail scissors...She wore wire-rimmed glasses she had owned since 1993...She weighed a hundred and eight pounds'
redundancy_findings.problem: Agnes's gray hair, wire-rimmed glasses, and small size are established in Ch1 L41 and partially re-stated in Ch3 L403. Ch8 L1583 re-describes all three traits again from Agnes's own POV, replicating the Ch1 introduction rather than adding new physical information.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch8 L1583, keep the biographical details that are genuinely new (cutting her own hair with nail scissors, glasses since 1993, weight stable for fifteen years) but fold them into action or reflection rather than a mirror-inventory that re-catalogs established traits. The nail-scissors detail and the '1993 glasses' age are new — foreground those. Cut the static re-description of 'gray hair short' and 'wire-rimmed glasses' as identifying features.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch8's self-inventory passage does not read as a fresh physical introduction. New biographical details are embedded in Agnes's routines rather than presented as a trait list.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch3 L433: 'Burt Kovacs was eighty-eight and his dementia was the real thing...his long fingers — musician's fingers'; Ch9 L2002: 'Burt Kovacs was eighty-eight and his dementia was real...those hands lay on the thermal blanket, long-fingered and mostly still, occasionally twitching in patterns that might have been remembered chord progressions'
redundancy_findings.problem: Near-verbatim restatement. Ch3 L433 establishes 'Burt Kovacs was eighty-eight and his dementia was the real thing' plus 'long fingers — musician's fingers.' Ch9 L2002 restates 'Burt Kovacs was eighty-eight and his dementia was real' plus 'long-fingered.' The sentence structure, age, dementia qualifier, and finger description are all repeated with minimal variation.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch9 L2002, cut the age-plus-dementia-qualifier sentence — the reader has been told this at least twice (Ch3 L433, Ch5 L946). Enter Burt's surveyor scene through what the surveyor sees, not through a narrator re-establishing his baseline. The twitching-as-chord-progressions detail is a fresh observation and can stay if the lead-in sentence is cut.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch9 does not contain a sentence of the form 'Burt Kovacs was [age] and his dementia was [real/the real thing].' Burt's condition is shown through behavior in the scene, not re-stated as narrator summary.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch9 L1858: 'moving through Wing A with the efficient speed of a physician who saw forty patients a day across three facilities and couldn't afford to linger'; Ch17 L4421: 'She was small and sharp and saw forty patients a day across three buildings'
redundancy_findings.problem: Dr. Prasad's defining trait — 'forty patients a day across three facilities' — is introduced in Ch9 L1858 and repeated near-verbatim in Ch17 L4421 ('forty patients a day across three buildings'). The only change is 'facilities' to 'buildings.'
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch17 L4421, cut the 'forty patients a day across three buildings' clause. Enter Prasad through what is different in this visit — her specific clinical actions, her reaction to Agnes and Earl — not through the same statistical summary used in Ch9.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch17's Prasad introduction does not repeat the forty-patients-across-three-facilities/buildings formula. Her workload is implied by her pace, not re-stated.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1 L41: 'small and thin, gray hair cut short, wire-rimmed glasses'; Ch3 L403: same; Ch8 L1583: same; Ch17 L4092: 'She was a small woman, five-two, gray hair cut short because she could manage it without help, wire-rimmed glasses reflecting the hallway's fluorescent light'
redundancy_findings.problem: Agnes is re-introduced in Ch17 L4092 with the same trait trio (small, gray hair cut short, wire-rimmed glasses) that was established in Ch1 L41 and repeated in Ch3 and Ch8. By the 4th repetition of this exact descriptor cluster, the reader does not need it.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch17 L4092, cut the physical re-inventory entirely. Agnes is 16 chapters into the novel — the reader knows what she looks like. Enter the moment through Agnes's expression, posture, or silence.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch17 L4092 does not list Agnes's height, hair color/style, or glasses as identifying features. She is recognized through action or expression.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1 L57: 'wispy white hair pressed flat on one side...long and thin'; Ch3 L433: 'wispy white hair...long fingers — musician's fingers'; Ch9 L2002: 'long-fingered'; Ch18 L4294: 'his wispy white hair pressed flat against the pillow, his long musician's hands slack on the blanket'
redundancy_findings.problem: Burt's 'wispy white hair' + 'long musician's hands/fingers' pairing appears for the 4th time in Ch18 L4294. The phrasing closely echoes Ch1 L57 ('wispy white hair pressed flat on one side').
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch18 L4294, replace the physical re-inventory with Burt's current state — the decline, the stillness, the post-concussion absence. One image is enough to place him; choose something that shows how he has changed rather than re-describing what has been constant.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch18's Burt description does not repeat the wispy-white-hair-plus-long-musician's-fingers pairing. His deterioration is shown through new observation.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch3 L393: 'The TV room was at the far end of Wing A, past the supply closet and the handicapped bathroom, as far from the station as the building allowed'; Ch5 L854: 'The TV room was at the far end of the wing, past the supply closet and the handicapped bathroom, the farthest common area from the nurse's station'
redundancy_findings.problem: Near-verbatim setting re-description. Ch3 L393 establishes the TV room's location using the 'supply closet and handicapped bathroom' landmarks. Ch5 L854 repeats the same landmarks with near-identical sentence structure. The reader has already been oriented to this space in Ch2 L330 and Ch3 L393.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch5 L854, delete the location re-establishment. Enter the scene through Carmen's emotional state or the operational insight ('Not visible from Devon's desk. That was the point.') — which is the new information. Lead with that.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch5's TV room entry does not repeat the supply-closet-and-handicapped-bathroom landmarks or the 'far end of the wing' positional phrase.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Earl's cough described in Ch3 L468, Ch5 L1088, Ch9 L1990/2186, Ch10 L2284/2463/2688/2700, Ch13 L3146/3184, Ch15 L3514-3676 (8+ instances), Ch16 L3757/3885, Ch17 L4128/4148/4162. By Ch17, the cough has been described 25+ times.
redundancy_findings.problem: Earl's declining health via the cough is the most aggressively re-proved baseline in the manuscript (32+ total instances). Ch17 adds 3 more instances after Ch15 already devoted an entire interior chapter to the cough. By Ch17, each additional description returns diminishing emotional weight.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch17, retain at most 1 cough instance — the strongest one that does new narrative work. Cut the other 2. The cough at L4162 ('heavier this time') has escalation value; the others at L4128 and L4148 are redundant.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch17 contains no more than 1 description of Earl's cough. That instance shows escalation or consequence, not merely the same wet-rattle sound.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch18 L4294: 'faint wet rattle that had been there for months'; L4393: 'whistle at the bottom of each inhale'; L4439: 'deep, liquid, wrong'; L4475: 'wet and deep enough to carry'. Four instances in the final chapter after 28+ prior.
redundancy_findings.problem: After 28+ prior instances, Ch18 adds 4 more cough descriptions. The L4439 instance (Dr. Prasad's examination) serves a diagnostic function; the others are atmospheric repetitions of a fully established condition.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch18, keep the L4439 cough (Prasad's exam) and at most 1 other. Cut the remaining 2. The reader does not need 'wet rattle' or 'whistle at the bottom of each inhale' restated after 28 prior descriptions.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch18 contains no more than 2 descriptions of Earl's cough. One is the medical examination; the other provides atmospheric presence without synonymous restatement of prior language.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch3 L433: 'Burt Kovacs was eighty-eight and his dementia was the real thing'; Ch5 L946: 'Burt's eighty-eight. He's got actual dementia — not like me, actual'; Ch9 L2002: 'Burt Kovacs was eighty-eight and his dementia was real'; Ch11 L2750: 'his dementia was real, had always been real, the only one in the ring who wasn't performing'
redundancy_findings.problem: The sentence 'Burt's dementia is real/the real thing' is restated in 4 chapters (Ch3, Ch5, Ch9, Ch11). The contrast with the ring's performances is identical each time. By Ch11, the reader has been told this 4 times.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch11 L2750, cut the 'his dementia was real, had always been real' clause. Show the change the fall has caused without re-asserting the baseline the reader already holds. The new information is the post-concussion deterioration; foreground that.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch11 does not re-assert that Burt's dementia is 'real' or 'the real thing' as a contrast to the ring's performances. His condition is shown through changed behavior.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Lola's gold crucifix appears 25+ times across 14 chapters. Ch16 alone has 6 references: L3755 'gold crucifix she never took off,' L3843 'crucifix caught the light,' L3849 'crucifix kept tapping against her thumbnail,' L3873 'holding her crucifix,' L3875 'working the chain through her fingers,' L3893 'crucifix was shaking in her grip.'
redundancy_findings.problem: The gold crucifix functions as physical descriptor and emotional-gesture prop. At 25+ instances manuscript-wide, it exceeds the 20-instance image-density threshold. Ch16 is the densest chapter with 6 references.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch16, reduce crucifix references from 6 to at most 2 — one at the chapter opening and one at the emotional climax. Vary Lola's stress-gesture vocabulary: she can grip the chair arm, press her hands together, or go still. For the manuscript overall, target keeping 8-10 of the strongest instances.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch16 contains no more than 2 crucifix references. Lola's emotional reactions in the other 4 instances use different physical gestures.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Lola's gold crucifix in Ch13: L3048 'gold crucifix at her throat,' L3172 'working the crucifix,' L3190 'thumb stopped moving on the crucifix,' L3232 'one hand on the crucifix.' Four instances in a single chapter.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch13 has 4 crucifix references adding to the manuscript's 25+ total. These all serve the same emotional function (Lola's anxiety during the Marcus confrontation) but use the same prop each time.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch13, reduce crucifix references from 4 to 1 — the most dramatically weighted instance. Replace the other 3 with varied physical behavior: Lola's tremor, her grip on the chair, her posture shift, or her silence.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch13 contains no more than 1 crucifix reference. Lola's emotional state in the remaining scenes is conveyed through different physical details.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Harry's 'faded blue cardigan' appears 11 times across 9 chapters: Ch1 L101/105, Ch3 L399, Ch5 L884, Ch9 L1874, Ch10 L2119, Ch11 L2780, Ch16 L3699, Ch17 L4018/4228, Ch18 L4483. Seven include a narrator gloss ('the one he wore to every meeting/interaction').
redundancy_findings.problem: The faded blue cardigan exceeds the 10-instance image-density threshold. Seven instances include a narrator gloss re-teaching the reader it is Harry's signature item. First establishment is Ch1 L101.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Across the manuscript, keep 4-6 cardigan references — the first establishment (Ch1), one mid-novel instance, and 1-2 in the final act. In every retained instance after Ch1, cut the 'he wore to every meeting' gloss. In Ch10 L2119, cut the gloss entirely.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: The 'faded blue cardigan' phrase appears no more than 6 times in the manuscript. No instance after Ch1 includes a narrator explanation that he wears it to every meeting or interaction.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch15 has 39 em-dash-containing prose lines out of 82 total prose lines = 47.6% density. Manuscript-wide density is 422/2269 = 18.6% (threshold 10%). Ch15 is nearly 5x the threshold.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch15 (Earl's interior chapter) uses em-dashes in nearly half of all prose lines. The density creates a rhythmic monotony where parenthetical asides and appositional clauses are structurally identical sentence after sentence. The em-dash is doing the work of commas, colons, semicolons, and periods, flattening the punctuation palette.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce Ch15's em-dash density from 47.6% to below 15% by converting the weakest em-dash constructions to commas, colons, or separate sentences. Prioritize cutting em-dashes that bracket purely descriptive asides where a comma or period would serve. Retain em-dashes only where the interruption creates genuine rhetorical force or captures Earl's interior rhythm.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch15 contains em-dashes in no more than 15% of prose lines (approximately 12 of 82). The retained em-dashes serve distinct rhetorical purposes not achievable with other punctuation.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch11 has 37 em-dash-containing prose lines out of 99 total prose lines = 37.4% density. Manuscript-wide threshold is 10%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch11 (post-Burt's-fall) uses em-dashes in over a third of prose lines, creating the same rhythmic saturation as Ch15. The appositional em-dash becomes the default sentence modifier rather than a marked choice.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce Ch11's em-dash density from 37.4% to below 15% by converting parenthetical asides, appositional restatements, and descriptive interruptions to commas, colons, or periods. Keep em-dashes for the sharpest narrative interruptions.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch11 contains em-dashes in no more than 15% of prose lines (approximately 15 of 99).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch9 has 47 em-dash-containing prose lines out of 142 total prose lines = 33.1% density. Manuscript-wide threshold is 10%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch9 (the state survey chapter) has the highest absolute count of em-dash lines (47). At 33.1% density, the em-dash functions as a general-purpose connector rather than a marked interruption.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce Ch9's em-dash density from 33.1% to below 15% by converting at least 25 of the 47 em-dash lines to use alternative punctuation. Prioritize cutting em-dashes in descriptive passages and background exposition; retain them in dialogue and high-tension moments where the interruption mirrors character thought.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch9 contains em-dashes in no more than 15% of prose lines (approximately 21 of 142).

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch1 L41: Agnes's roommate is 'Mrs. Nowak'; Ch2 L266: Agnes's roommate is 'a woman named Dorothy who hadn't spoken in eleven months and received nutrition through a PEG tube'; Ch8 L1571: 'Her roommate, Doris Okonkwo, eighty-three, was five years into Alzheimer's'; Ch8+ uses 'Doris' consistently (L1577, 1705, 1721, 1815, 1914, 2110); Ch18 L4361/4423/4465/4595 uses 'Doris'
consistency_findings.problem: Agnes's roommate in Room 108A is given three different names in the first 8 chapters: Mrs. Nowak (Ch1 L41), Dorothy (Ch2 L266), and Doris Okonkwo (Ch8 L1571). All three descriptions refer to a woman with advanced dementia in Agnes's room. The name stabilizes as 'Doris' from Ch8 onward but the Ch1 and Ch2 names are contradictory.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Standardize Agnes's roommate name to 'Doris Okonkwo' throughout. In Ch1 L41, replace 'Mrs. Nowak' with 'Doris' or 'Mrs. Okonkwo.' In Ch2 L266, replace 'Dorothy' with 'Doris.' Verify all subsequent references maintain the same name.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Agnes's roommate is named consistently in every chapter. No instance of 'Mrs. Nowak' or 'Dorothy' remains in the manuscript when referring to this character.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch17 L4058: 'Her roommate, Mrs. Delacroix, was asleep and snoring wetly in the bed nearest the door'; Ch18 L4361: 'Her roommate, Doris, was snoring in the next bed'; but Mrs. Delacroix is established as the Room 110 patient from Ch4 L685 onward: 'Room 110 — Mrs. Delacroix — produced the same low, continuous murmur'
consistency_findings.problem: In Ch17 L4058, Agnes's roommate in 108A is called 'Mrs. Delacroix.' But Mrs. Delacroix is already established as the Room 110 moaning patient (Ch4 L685, Ch4 L745, Ch4 L781, Ch5 L852, etc.). This creates a name collision: two different patients in different rooms sharing the same name. Ch18 L4361 reverts to 'Doris' for Agnes's roommate, confirming the Ch17 usage is an error.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch17 L4058, replace 'Mrs. Delacroix' with 'Doris' to match the established name for Agnes's roommate. Mrs. Delacroix should remain exclusively the Room 110 patient.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Ch17 L4058 refers to Agnes's roommate as 'Doris,' not 'Mrs. Delacroix.' Mrs. Delacroix appears only in references to Room 110.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch1 L11: Room 110 patient is 'Mr. Delvecchio was ninety-one and contracted into a permanent C-shape'; Ch2 L308: Room 110 patient is 'Mrs. Davila moaned through the night'; Ch4 L685: Room 110 patient is 'Mrs. Delacroix — produced the same low, continuous murmur'
consistency_findings.problem: The Room 110 moaning patient changes name three times: Mr. Delvecchio (Ch1 L11), Mrs. Davila (Ch2 L308), and Mrs. Delacroix (Ch4 L685 onward). All three are described as producing the same continuous moaning/murmuring sound. Ch2's 'Mrs. Davila' appears only once and contradicts both the Ch1 and Ch4+ names.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Standardize the Room 110 patient name. If Mr. Delvecchio dies or is transferred between Ch1 and Ch4 (plausible in a nursing home), make that transition explicit with a brief narrative mention. If the same patient is intended throughout, pick one name — 'Mrs. Delacroix' is used most consistently (Ch4-Ch18) — and update Ch1 and Ch2 accordingly. In Ch2 L308, replace 'Mrs. Davila' with the chosen name.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The Room 110 moaning patient has a single consistent name throughout, or any name change is accompanied by a narrative acknowledgment of a new patient.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch3 L433: 'He might have been dreaming about a piano'; Ch9 L2002: 'his hands moving over piano keys fast and exact, as if the thinking lived in his fingers'; Ch9 L2018: 'rubbing his thumb against his forefinger...as if there were still piano keys there'; Ch11 L2710: 'those long musician's fingers, the ones that had held a trumpet in a photograph she'd seen a hundred times'; Ch11 L2714: 'him holding a trumpet'; Ch11 L2750: 'playing trumpet in clubs on Fifty-Second Street'; continuity sheet: 'Retired jazz musician and trumpeter'
consistency_findings.problem: Burt's instrument is inconsistent. Ch3 and Ch9 describe him dreaming of piano keys and his hands moving over piano keys. Ch11 and the continuity sheet identify him as a trumpet player who played clubs on 52nd Street. A trumpeter's muscle memory would not produce piano-key finger movements. The finger-tapping gesture (tapping on armrests) is ambiguous but the 'piano keys' language in Ch9 L2002 and L2018 directly contradicts the trumpet identification.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch9 L2002, replace 'piano keys' with a trumpet-appropriate description (e.g., 'his hands holding a horn, his fingers pressing valves'). In Ch9 L2018, replace 'as if there were still piano keys there' with a trumpet-appropriate gesture. In Ch3 L433, replace 'dreaming about a piano' with 'dreaming about a trumpet' or 'dreaming about a stage.' Ensure all instrument references align with the continuity sheet's 'trumpeter' designation.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: No passage describes Burt's fingers on piano keys. All instrument references identify him as a trumpet player consistent with the continuity sheet.
